subject: ENGLISH
title: Whose Is It?
objective: Use possessive pronouns to show ownership.
introduction: Mine, yours, his, hers, ours, and theirs can stand alone and replace a possessive noun phrase. You will explore examples, sort cards, practice, and answer five questions.

vocabulary.definition: A key word used in the lesson “Whose Is It?”.
A key word used in the lesson “Whose Is It?”.
A key word used in the lesson “Whose Is It?”.

activities.instruction: Study the idea and listen to the narration.
activities.content: Mine, yours, his, hers, ours, and theirs can stand alone and replace a possessive noun phrase.
activities.accessibilityAlternative: Text alternative: Mine, yours, his, hers, ours, and theirs can stand alone and replace a possessive noun phrase.

activities.instruction: Explore each learning token, then read the examples.
activities.accessibilityAlternative: A numbered list of the same examples is available for keyboard and screen-reader use.

activities.instruction: Sort each card into “fits the lesson” or “does not fit.”

doesNotFit: That pencil is her when standing alone

feedback.correct: Your groups follow the lesson rule.
feedback.retry: Use the explanation, not color or position.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: Tap an item and then tap a labeled category; dragging is optional.

feedback.retry: Read the lesson rule again. The kite is her. and The hers kite. do not fit.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: All choices are visible text and selectable with TalkBack.

activities.instruction: Match each example to the lesson idea.

feedback.retry: Return to the explanation and compare one pair at a time.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: A list-based matching control replaces drag gestures.

activities.instruction: Complete the guided review in order.

feedback.correct: You are ready for the five-question check.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: Up and down buttons provide an alternative to dragging.

items.prompt: Which statement best matches “Whose Is It?”?

options.text: Mine, yours, his, hers, ours, and theirs can stand alone and replace a possessive noun phrase.
That pencil is her when standing alone

items.explanation: The correct response is “Mine, yours, his, hers, ours, and theirs can stand alone and replace a possessive noun phrase.” because it follows the lesson explanation: Mine, yours, his, hers, ours, and theirs can stand alone and replace a possessive noun phrase.

options.text: That pencil is her when standing alone

items.explanation: The correct response is “The red bag is mine.” because it follows the lesson explanation: Mine, yours, his, hers, ours, and theirs can stand alone and replace a possessive noun phrase.

items.prompt: Which choice does not follow the lesson idea?
items.explanation: The correct response is “This is mine bag.” because it follows the lesson explanation: Mine, yours, his, hers, ours, and theirs can stand alone and replace a possessive noun phrase.
The correct response is “The kite is hers.” because it follows the lesson explanation: Mine, yours, his, hers, ours, and theirs can stand alone and replace a possessive noun phrase.

items.prompt: Which example should be used in the final review?
items.explanation: The correct response is “The blue hats are theirs.” because it follows the lesson explanation: Mine, yours, his, hers, ours, and theirs can stand alone and replace a possessive noun phrase.

assetSpecs.prompt: Friendly Grade 3 cartoon storybook concept board for Whose Is It?; rounded shapes, warm dark-brown outlines, tropical palette, no embedded text, uncluttered small-screen layout. Compose overlays provide labels and interactive targets.
assetSpecs.contentDescription: Five connected learning tokens for exploring examples in Whose Is It?.
